summary: Basecamp has raised its minimum pay to $70,000 for all employees, regardless of role or location. The company benchmarks salaries at the top 10% of San Francisco market rates, even though no employees live there. DHH explains that while this benchmark works well for programmers and designers, it didn't adequately lift pay for roles like customer support where tech hasn't raised market rates equally. The new floor creates a roughly 5x ratio between highest and lowest paid employees, which DHH frames as more egalitarian than typical tech companies. He positions this as an example for other companies while cautioning them to grow into such policies sustainably.
key_points: Basecamp pays SF top-10% rates to all employees regardless of location, making them extremely competitive in lower cost-of-living areas
Tech industry market rates disproportionately benefit programmers and designers while leaving support and admin roles behind
The $70,000 minimum breaks the market-rate peg for roles where benchmarks don't reflect adequate compensation
The entire company salary range is roughly 5x from lowest to highest paid, which is more compressed than typical tech companies
Sustainable profitability over 15 years enabled these policies — DHH warns against companies overextending to match venture-funded competitors
money_quotes: We pay at the top 10% of San Francisco market rates for salary + bonus at Basecamp. Those rates apply to everyone here, regardless of the role or where they actually live.
While we might be top 10% for San Francisco, I'm pretty sure we're in the top 1% for, say, Fenwick, Ontario or Spokane, Washington or Madrid, Spain.
Where before we'd have roles that started in the $50,000s, our new minimum wage at Basecamp is now $70,000, regardless of what the benchmarks say.
We're choosing to link 'minimum pay' not simply with what's the least we can get away with, but rather as literally just the lowest pay that we offer at Basecamp.
We can't fix all the problems in technology, whether around pay or otherwise. But we can set one example.
Like having the pay range of the entire company, not just the most sought-after positions, feel the rising wealth of technology.
Not because we're putting ourselves in a bind trying to keep up with Mr Venture and Mrs Capital.
key_insight: Tech companies that benchmark only to market rates perpetuate inequality across roles — profitable companies should deliberately break that peg to ensure all employees share in the industry's wealth.
